retrieved.title: Legalisation and translation
heading_path: How do my documents become translated?
retrieved.text: We only accept documents that were issued in German or English. If your documents were issued in a different language, a translation is required. Please regard the following terms: - If full diplomatic certification is required, your documents have to be legalised first. Only then they may be translated including all stamps, seals, and certification notes. - An apostille must also be translated. This means that the apostille has to be affixed to the original document first. Afterwards it can be translated. - The documents must be translated by official sworn and court certified translators. - The translation must be inseparably attached to the original document. Do not detach the documents (not even when scanning them!). Else the translation will be void. # FAQ / - Which documents require diplomatic certification? We require diplomatic certification of all documents that attest your prior education. - Bachelor/diploma programmes: All pages of your secondary school leaving certificate and, if applicable, transcripts of records/diplomas (if you have studied at a university before). - Master programmes: Your higher education degree certificate, relevant transcripts of records and/or the diploma supplement. - My documents have not been diplomatically certified yet. Can I still apply for admission? No, as you may only apply once all documents have been diplomatically certified and translated, as required. retrieved.title: Legalisation and translation
retrieved.text:
1. Inform yourself in the issuing country which national public authority may issue an apostille, and if there are any requirements.
2. Have an apostille added to your documents.
   The apostille can be added on the document itself, on an attached page, or by means of an electronic apostille. Do not detach the apostille! The apostille must attest any signature(s) by the issuing educational institution (directorate, rectorate, or deanship) that the document bears. The signature of the notary or translation agency may not be attested.
3. Have your documents translated.
   Your [documents as well as the apostille has to be translated](#c1122153), if they were not issued in German or English.
4. Apply for admission to a study programme.
   Scan all pages of your document, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

Documents issued in certain countries do not need to be diplomatically certified. However, we require an [official translation in German or English](#c1122153), if the documents were not issued in either of these languages.

Documents issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland are exempt from all legalisation requirements.

retrieved.title: Legalisation and translation
retrieved.text:
You wish to apply for admission and your documents were issued in a non-EU/EEA country? Please have them translated after they were diplomatically certified (i.e. legalised) first.

Your documents were issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland? Such documents need only [be translated](#c1122153), while legalisation is not required.

There are four possibilities:

- [Full diplomatic certification](#c1121827)
- [Apostille](#c1121828)
- [Diplomatic certification is not required](#c1121829)
- [Diplomatic certification is temporarily deferred](#c1121830)

retrieved.title: Legalisation and translation
heading_path: Full diplomatic certification
retrieved.text:
1. **Inform yourself in the issuing country about the national public authority responsible for legalising documents.**
   The process of legalisation usually begins at the ministry of education, followed by the ministry for foreign affairs. Finally, the appropriate Austrian representation authority (embassy or consulate) issues an over-legalisation. In some countries, in lieu of the ministry of foreign affairs, the ministry of justice is responsible for legalising documents.
2. **Have the appropriate ministries certify your documents by means of the respective seals and attestation clauses.**
3. **Present your documents to the Austrian representation authorities.**
   Schedule an appointment with the [appropriate representation authority](https://www.bmeia.gv.at/en/embassies-consulates/search-for-austrian-representations) and provide them with your legalised documents. The embassy or consulate will issue an over-legalisation.
4. **Have your documents translated.**
   Your [documents as well as all seals and attestation clauses they contain have to be translated](#c1122153), if they were not issued in German or English.
5. **Apply for admission to a study programme**
   Scan all pages of your documents, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

Your documents have to be legalised by [apostille](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/#c1121828) and must be translated into either German or English afterwards.

Your steps in the admission procedure depend on the degree programme of your choice.

/

- Bachelor/diploma programme

  If your secondary-school leaving certificate (12 years of education) is from India and you have achieved at least 85% of the maximum possible marks, your application can be processed. Each application must be reviewed individually.

  [Detailed information about admission to a bachelor or diploma programme](/en/admission-procedure/bachelordiploma-programmes/)
- Master programme

  Your application can be processed if:

  - You have completed a bachelor programme with a stipulated duration of at least three years (full-time) at a recognised university AND
  - your university is classified as Section 1 or 2 (according to ENIC NARIC AUSTRIA Credential Evaluation) AND
  - you have achieved at least 60% of the maximum points available in your studies.

  OR, if:

  - You have completed a bachelor programme with a stipulated duration of at least three years (full-time) at a recognised university AND
  - you have completed your bachelor programme at a state university, which is classified as Section 3 (according to ENIC NARIC AUSTRIA Credential Evaluation) AND
  - you have achieved at least 80% of the maximum points available in your studies.

  Each application must be reviewed individually.

  [Detailed information about admission to master programmes](/en/admission-procedure/master-programmes/)

retrieved.title: I am from ... and want to study at the University of Vienna. What do I have to keep in mind?
heading_path: I want to study a bachelor/diploma programme
General requirements for the application for admission to bachelor/diploma programmes
retrieved.text:
- scan of your valid your passport or personal ID card (front and back!) as JPEG or PNG file
- all pages (!) of your original secondary-school leaving certificate
  - including the required [legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) according to the issuing country
  - plus sworn translation (if not issued in German or English)
- [list of subjects](/en/admission/bachelordiploma-programmes/list-of-subjects/) in its original version
  - including the required [legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) according to the issuing country
  - together with a sworn translation (if not issued in German or English)
- if you have already studied: transcripts/diplomas in their original version
  - including the required [legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) according to the issuing country
  - plus sworn translation (if not issued in German or English)
- for German and multilingual programmes: [proof of German language proficiency](/en/admission/german-language-proficiency/) at level A2 or higher
- for the bachelor programmes in International Legal Studies and Mathematical Foundations of Data Science: [proof of English language proficiency](/en/admission/english-language-proficiency/) at level B2 or higher

You have to submit the application for admission within the [application period](/en/admission/application-and-admission-periods/). The application period depends on your citizenship. Please note: [degree programmes with entrance exam procedure](/en/entrance-exam/) have a special application period.

reranked.title: Legalisation and translation
heading_path: How do my documents become translated?
reranked.text: We only accept documents that were issued in German or English. If your documents were issued in a different language, a translation is required. Please regard the following terms: - If full diplomatic certification is required, your documents have to be legalised first. Only then they may be translated including all stamps, seals, and certification notes. - An apostille must also be translated. This means that the apostille has to be affixed to the original document first. Afterwards it can be translated. - The documents must be translated by official sworn and court certified translators. - The translation must be inseparably attached to the original document. Do not detach the documents (not even when scanning them!). Else the translation will be void. # FAQ / - Which documents require diplomatic certification? We require diplomatic certification of all documents that attest your prior education. - Bachelor/diploma programmes: All pages of your secondary school leaving certificate and, if applicable, transcripts of records/diplomas (if you have studied at a university before). - Master programmes: Your higher education degree certificate, relevant transcripts of records and/or the diploma supplement. - My documents have not been diplomatically certified yet. Can I still apply for admission? No, as you may only apply once all documents have been diplomatically certified and translated, as required. If your application is incomplete, it may prolong its lead time. - For how

reranked.title: Legalisation and translation
reranked.text:
1. Inform yourself in the issuing country which national public authority may issue an apostille, and if there are any requirements.
2. Have an apostille added to your documents.
   The apostille can be added on the document itself, on an attached page, or by means of an electronic apostille. Do not detach the apostille! The apostille must attest any signature(s) by the issuing educational institution (directorate, rectorate, or deanship) that the document bears. The signature of the notary or translation agency may not be attested.
3. Have your documents translated.
   Your [documents as well as the apostille has to be translated](#c1122153), if they were not issued in German or English.
4. Apply for admission to a study programme.
   Scan all pages of your document, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

Documents issued in certain countries do not need to be diplomatically certified. However, we require an [official translation in German or English](#c1122153), if the documents were not issued in either of these languages.

Documents issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland are exempt from all legalisation requirements.

reranked.title: Legalisation and translation
reranked.text:
You wish to apply for admission and your documents were issued in a non-EU/EEA country? Please have them translated after they were diplomatically certified (i.e. legalised) first.

Your documents were issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland? Such documents need only [be translated](#c1122153), while legalisation is not required.

There are four possibilities:

- [Full diplomatic certification](#c1121827)
- [Apostille](#c1121828)
- [Diplomatic certification is not required](#c1121829)
- [Diplomatic certification is temporarily deferred](#c1121830)

reranked.title: ABC of terminology
heading_path: L
reranked.text: Legalisation Documents required for admission must be legalised if they were issued in a non-EU/EEA country. Depending on the country of issuance, two types of legalisation are possible: an apostille or a full legalisation. Related Links: - [Information regarding the apostille](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) - [Information about full legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) Related entries: - [Apostille](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31900&cHash=b73f3e20226f1cd8a29d5f74338e38cf) - [Full legalisation](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31996&cHash=b2d27c84616f8a39b0d4f3ad8770c2f8) - List of subjects If your secondary-school leaving certificate is from a non-EU/EEA country, you need a list of subjects to apply for admission to a bachelor/diploma programme. In many countries, the list of subjects is part of the school-leaving certificate. It shows how many hours of a subject you have completed in the course of your schooling. Related links: - [List of subjects](/en/admission/bachelordiploma-programmes/list-of-subjects/) - Livestreaming Teachers who hold courses in u:stream lecture halls can offer their courses as a live stream. There are two types of live streams: a web stream or a room2room stream. Audio and video recordings can be made available to the students on the e-learning platform (=Moodle). Related Links: - [Recording courses – u:stream](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/ustream/) - [E-learning platform Moodle](https://moodle.univie.ac.at) Related entries: - [Moodle](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31195&cHash=f09a4dd9dabecf19aab1edecd36ac050)

reranked.title: Legalisation and translation
heading_path: Full diplomatic certification
reranked.text:
1. **Inform yourself in the issuing country about the national public authority responsible for legalising documents.**
   The process of legalisation usually begins at the ministry of education, followed by the ministry for foreign affairs. Finally, the appropriate Austrian representation authority (embassy or consulate) issues an over-legalisation. In some countries, in lieu of the ministry of foreign affairs, the ministry of justice is responsible for legalising documents.
2. **Have the appropriate ministries certify your documents by means of the respective seals and attestation clauses.**
3. **Present your documents to the Austrian representation authorities.**
   Schedule an appointment with the [appropriate representation authority](https://www.bmeia.gv.at/en/embassies-consulates/search-for-austrian-representations) and provide them with your legalised documents. The embassy or consulate will issue an over-legalisation.
4. **Have your documents translated.**
   Your [documents as well as all seals and attestation clauses they contain have to be translated](#c1122153), if they were not issued in German or English.
5. **Apply for admission to a study programme**
   Scan all pages of your documents, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

reranked.title: Competence check at VFS Global
heading_path: Step 1: Inform yourself about the admission requirements
reranked.text:
Your documents have to be legalised by [apostille](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/#c1121828) and must be translated into either German or English afterwards.

Your steps in the admission procedure depend on the degree programme of your choice.

/

- Bachelor/diploma programme

  If your secondary-school leaving certificate (12 years of education) is from India and you have achieved at least 85% of the maximum possible marks, your application can be processed. Each application must be reviewed individually.

  [Detailed information about admission to a bachelor or diploma programme](/en/admission-procedure/bachelordiploma-programmes/)
- Master programme

  Your application can be processed if:

  - You have completed a bachelor programme with a stipulated duration of at least three years (full-time) at a recognised university AND
  - your university is classified as Section 1 or 2 (according to ENIC NARIC AUSTRIA Credential Evaluation) AND
  - you have achieved at least 60% of the maximum points available in your studies.

  OR, if:

  - You have completed a bachelor programme with a stipulated duration of at least three years (full-time) at a recognised university AND
  - you have completed your bachelor programme at a state university, which is classified as Section 3 (according to ENIC NARIC AUSTRIA Credential Evaluation) AND
  - you have achieved at least 80% of the maximum points available in your studies.

  Each application must be reviewed individually.

  [Detailed information about admission to master programmes](/en/admission-procedure/master-programmes/)

selected.title: Legalisation and translation
heading_path: How do my documents become translated?
selected.text: We only accept documents that were issued in German or English. If your documents were issued in a different language, a translation is required. Please regard the following terms: - If full diplomatic certification is required, your documents have to be legalised first. Only then they may be translated including all stamps, seals, and certification notes. - An apostille must also be translated. This means that the apostille has to be affixed to the original document first. Afterwards it can be translated. - The documents must be translated by official sworn and court certified translators. - The translation must be inseparably attached to the original document. Do not detach the documents (not even when scanning them!). Else the translation will be void. # FAQ / - Which documents require diplomatic certification? We require diplomatic certification of all documents that attest your prior education. - Bachelor/diploma programmes: All pages of your secondary school leaving certificate and, if applicable, transcripts of records/diplomas (if you have studied at a university before). - Master programmes: Your higher education degree certificate, relevant transcripts of records and/or the diploma supplement. - My documents have not been diplomatically certified yet. Can I still apply for admission? No, as you may only apply once all documents have been diplomatically certified and translated, as required. If your application is incomplete, it may prolong its lead time. - For how

selected.title: Legalisation and translation
selected.text:
1. Inform yourself in the issuing country which national public authority may issue an apostille, and if there are any requirements.
2. Have an apostille added to your documents.
   The apostille can be added on the document itself, on an attached page, or by means of an electronic apostille. Do not detach the apostille! The apostille must attest any signature(s) by the issuing educational institution (directorate, rectorate, or deanship) that the document bears. The signature of the notary or translation agency may not be attested.
3. Have your documents translated.
   Your [documents as well as the apostille has to be translated](#c1122153), if they were not issued in German or English.
4. Apply for admission to a study programme.
   Scan all pages of your document, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

Documents issued in certain countries do not need to be diplomatically certified. However, we require an [official translation in German or English](#c1122153), if the documents were not issued in either of these languages.

Documents issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland are exempt from all legalisation requirements.

selected.title: Legalisation and translation
selected.text:
You wish to apply for admission and your documents were issued in a non-EU/EEA country? Please have them translated after they were diplomatically certified (i.e. legalised) first.

Your documents were issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland? Such documents need only [be translated](#c1122153), while legalisation is not required.

There are four possibilities:

- [Full diplomatic certification](#c1121827)
- [Apostille](#c1121828)
- [Diplomatic certification is not required](#c1121829)
- [Diplomatic certification is temporarily deferred](#c1121830)

heading_path: L
selected.text: Legalisation Documents required for admission must be legalised if they were issued in a non-EU/EEA country. Depending on the country of issuance, two types of legalisation are possible: an apostille or a full legalisation. prompt_messages.content:
You are a concise assistant for prospective and current University of Vienna students. Answer the question using ONLY the numbered sources provided.
Rules:
- Base every statement on the sources; never add outside knowledge or guess.
- Cite each claim inline with the bracket number(s) of the source(s) you used, e.g. [1] or [2][3]. Cite only sources you actually relied on.
- If the sources do not contain the answer, reply that you don't know, and do NOT cite any sources.
- Keep the answer short and direct (it may be read aloud by a voice assistant).

Sources:
[1] Legalisation and translation — How do my documents become translated?
We only accept documents that were issued in German or English. If your documents were issued in a different language, a translation is required. Please regard the following terms: - If full diplomatic certification is required, your documents have to be legalised first. Only then they may be translated including all stamps, seals, and certification notes. - An apostille must also be translated. This means that the apostille has to be affixed to the original document first. Afterwards it can be translated. - The documents must be translated by official sworn and court certified translators. - The translation must be inseparably attached to the original document. Do not detach the documents (not even when scanning them!). Else the translation will be void. # FAQ / - Which documents require diplomatic certification? We require diplomatic certification of all documents that attest your prior education. - Bachelor/diploma programmes: All pages of your secondary school leaving certificate and, if applicable, transcripts of records/diplomas (if you have studied at a university before). - Master programmes: Your higher education degree certificate, relevant transcripts of records and/or the diploma supplement. - My documents have not been diplomatically certified yet. Can I still apply for admission? No, as you may only apply once all documents have been diplomatically certified and translated, as required. If your application is incomplete, it may prolong its lead time. - For how

[2] Legalisation and translation
1. Inform yourself in the issuing country which national public authority may issue an apostille, and if there are any requirements.
2. Have an apostille added to your documents.
   The apostille can be added on the document itself, on an attached page, or by means of an electronic apostille. Do not detach the apostille! The apostille must attest any signature(s) by the issuing educational institution (directorate, rectorate, or deanship) that the document bears. The signature of the notary or translation agency may not be attested.
3. Have your documents translated.
   Your [documents as well as the apostille has to be translated](#c1122153), if they were not issued in German or English.
4. Apply for admission to a study programme.
   Scan all pages of your document, including all seals and (if necessary) the translation. After assuring yourself that the scan is legible, combine your documents into a PDF-file. Follow the next steps in the [admission procedure](/en/admission-procedure/).

Documents issued in certain countries do not need to be diplomatically certified. However, we require an [official translation in German or English](#c1122153), if the documents were not issued in either of these languages.

Documents issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland are exempt from all legalisation requirements.

[3] Legalisation and translation
You wish to apply for admission and your documents were issued in a non-EU/EEA country? Please have them translated after they were diplomatically certified (i.e. legalised) first.

Your documents were issued in an EU/EEA country or Switzerland? Such documents need only [be translated](#c1122153), while legalisation is not required.

There are four possibilities:

- [Full diplomatic certification](#c1121827)
- [Apostille](#c1121828)
- [Diplomatic certification is not required](#c1121829)
- [Diplomatic certification is temporarily deferred](#c1121830)

[4] ABC of terminology — L
Legalisation Documents required for admission must be legalised if they were issued in a non-EU/EEA country. Depending on the country of issuance, two types of legalisation are possible: an apostille or a full legalisation. Related Links: - [Information regarding the apostille](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) - [Information about full legalisation](/en/admission/info-third-country-students/legalisation/) Related entries: - [Apostille](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31900&cHash=b73f3e20226f1cd8a29d5f74338e38cf) - [Full legalisation](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31996&cHash=b2d27c84616f8a39b0d4f3ad8770c2f8) - List of subjects If your secondary-school leaving certificate is from a non-EU/EEA country, you need a list of subjects to apply for admission to a bachelor/diploma programme. In many countries, the list of subjects is part of the school-leaving certificate. It shows how many hours of a subject you have completed in the course of your schooling. Related links: - [List of subjects](/en/admission/bachelordiploma-programmes/list-of-subjects/) - Livestreaming Teachers who hold courses in u:stream lecture halls can offer their courses as a live stream. There are two types of live streams: a web stream or a room2room stream. Audio and video recordings can be made available to the students on the e-learning platform (=Moodle). Related Links: - [Recording courses – u:stream](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/ustream/) - [E-learning platform Moodle](https://moodle.univie.ac.at) Related entries: - [Moodle](/en/abc-of-terminology/related-entries/?tx_irfaq_pi1%5BshowUid%5D=31195&cHash=f09a4dd9dabecf19aab1edecd36ac050)

Question: applying from outside europe, kinda lost — what docs do i actually need, like does my school certificate need that apostille/legalisation and a translation?

generate.raw_response: If you're applying from outside Europe, you need to check the specific requirements for your country [5][6]. Generally, documents issued in a non-EU/EEA country require legalisation, which can be either an apostille [2] or full diplomatic certification [1]. Your school certificate needs to be legalised and translated into German or English if it's not already in one of these languages [1][2]. The apostille or certification must be attached to the original document, and then the entire document, including the apostille, must be translated [2].
